value: gm-method lesson (2026-07-03): when asked to 'pick the best ideas from N papers/crates/repos and implement them', the productive move is NOT a literature survey followed by generic additions -- it's mapping each source's core mechanism onto a SPECIFIC gap already witnessed in the target codebase via codesearch, then implementing only the mapped subset. Process: (1) WebFetch each source, extract core mechanism in 2-4 sentences (retry with a resolved/canonical URL if the given one 404s or returns binary-PDF garbage -- pypdf via exec beats a WebFetch that can't parse PDF binary); (2) codesearch the target's actual hot path (e.g. a store's knn method) to find the real O(n)/missing-feature gap; (3) for each source idea, ask 'does this specific mechanism address that specific gap' -- reject ideas that don't map (e.g. skip literally forcing Hilbert-curve INTO an unrelated clusterer if it fits better as a retrieval-side index); (4) implement the minimal wiring, not a reimplementation of the whole paper's system (e.g. VStream's 4-level hot-cold disk tiering is real but out of scope for an in-memory store -- defer it explicitly rather than half-build it). CORNER-CASE DENSITY: each landed mechanism needs its own empty/dimension-mismatch/invalidation-on-mutation PRD rows, not just the happy path. WITNESS DISCIPLINE: prefer real exec_js/python snippets proving the mechanism works (e.g. 'bucketed candidate set still contains the true nearest neighbor') over trusting a full pytest pass alone -- weak/random encoders in a test env can mask whether a new ranking signal is actually contributing; isolate the mechanism (compare hybrid ON vs OFF scores directly) to prove causation, not just non-crash.
